props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Few-Shot Prompting: Implicit Style Encoding via Examples

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Few-shot works by adding completed input-output pairs before the target input so the model generalizes the pattern. A good set of three to five examples encodes length, vocabulary, structure, domain jargon, and edge-cases simultaneously. Example quality matters more than count; one bad example contaminates the whole generalization. Good examples are representative, internally consistent, and matched to the real target distribution.

Good examples are representative, internally consistent, and matched to the real target distribution.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Few-Shot Prompting: Implicit Style Encoding via Examples belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

beats.narration_text: The idea that examples compress five specifications into one pattern is what I want to test on a real task. Say I need Claude to write product blurbs in my brand voice: (1) show how three examples encode length, register, structure, jargon, and edge-cases at once versus five separate instruction paragraphs, (2) demonstrate how one off-distribution example contaminates the pattern, and (3) check my three examples against your representative, consistent, and target-matching rules.

props.segment: Few-Shot Prompting: Implicit Style Encoding via Examples
props.command: Some things are easier to show than to describe. Writing style is one of them. Few-shot prompting is implicit style encoding. Examples do more work per token than any instruction.

beats.narration_text: Few-shot examples work by adding completed input-output pairs to the prompt before the target input. The model generalizes from the pattern: what went in, what came out. Three to five examples is usually sufficient. The examples communicate format, tone, length, vocabulary, and domain conventions simultaneously — no separate instruction for each. The pattern is the prompt.

A well-chosen set of examples encodes five things at once: output length, vocabulary register, structural template, domain jargon, and edge-case handling. Encode them in prose instructions and you have five separate paragraphs. Encode them in three examples and you have three sentences that demonstrate all five simultaneously. Examples compress specification. Instruction expands it.

Example quality matters more than example count. One bad example contaminates the pattern. Three rules for good examples. First: examples must be representative — do not pick edge cases as your only examples. Second: examples must be internally consistent — mixed-style examples teach inconsistency. Third: examples must match the target distribution — if your real inputs are technical, your examples should be too. Garbage examples, garbage generalization.

Your turn. Pick a writing style you want Claude to match — a specific author, publication, or house style. Write three examples in that style, each under fifty words. Add a fourth blank example with only the input. Run it. Now add a single instruction describing the style and run without the examples. Compare. The example version is almost always more accurate. That is the compression advantage. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.

props.segment: Few-Shot Prompting: Implicit Style Encoding via Examples
props.command: Write three examples in a target style, each under 50 words. Add a blank target input. Run with examples, then with only a style instruction. Compare accuracy.
